sub_problems.description: Determine the core functional requirements for a website boilerplate
constraints: Must be generalizable across common website types

sub_problems.description: Define the technical architecture and technology stack
Establish development workflow and tooling configuration
Design the user interface foundation and accessibility baseline

constraints: Must be clear for new developers

assumptions.text: The boilerplate is intended for modern web development (not legacy systems)
assumptions.label: VERIFIED
assumptions.rationale: The Greek term 'ιστοσελίδες' (websites) implies contemporary web development context

assumptions.text: The boilerplate should be reusable across multiple projects
assumptions.label: VERIFIED
assumptions.rationale: The term 'boilerplate' inherently implies reusability and standardization

assumptions.text: The primary users are developers with web development experience
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: Boilerplates are typically developer tools, but skill level isn't specified

assumptions.text: The website will be publicly accessible on the internet
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN
assumptions.rationale: Could be for intranet or local applications; scope isn't specified

assumptions.text: Performance optimization is a priority requirement
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: Modern websites typically require performance considerations, but not explicitly stated

failure_modes: Over-engineering the boilerplate with unnecessary features that increase complexity
Making technology choices that become obsolete quickly in fast-moving web ecosystem
Creating documentation that doesn't match actual implementation or becomes outdated
Failing to account for accessibility requirements leading to exclusionary design
Building a boilerplate that's too specific to one use case, limiting reusability

candidates.content: The entire problem framing assumes a rational, predictable development environment that doesn't exist. The sub-problems treat website boilerplate creation as an engineering optimization problem when it's actually a political and cultural negotiation. SP1's 'generalizable across common website types' constraint is impossible—what's essential for an e-commerce site (payment integration, cart state) is bloat for a portfolio site. SP2's 'balance modernity with stability' is a moving target where today's modern stack (React 19, Next.js 15) becomes tomorrow's legacy code before the boilerplate is even adopted. SP3 assumes team collaboration needs can be predetermined, ignoring that boilerplates often outlive their original teams and get forked by solo developers. SP4's WCAG compliance is a legal minefield—different jurisdictions interpret guidelines differently, and automated accessibility checkers miss 70% of real issues. The assumption that 'primary users are developers with web development experience' is dangerously narrow—in reality, boilerplates get used by designers, marketing teams, and junior developers who copy-paste without understanding. The performance optimization hypothesis ignores that most performance bottlenecks come from third-party scripts (analytics, ads, social widgets) that developers add later, making any boilerplate-level optimization irrelevant.
key_insights: Boilerplates create vendor lock-in at the architectural level—once you choose a stack through SP2, migrating away requires rewriting the entire codebase, which teams rarely do until it's catastrophically broken.

The documentation problem (SP5) is fundamentally unsolvable because documentation decays three times faster than code—by the time a developer finds your boilerplate, the setup instructions are already outdated due to transitive dependency updates.

Accessibility (SP4) isn't a checkbox but a moving target—screen reader behavior varies across devices, browsers, and versions, making any 'baseline' compliance immediately obsolete with the next browser update.

candidates.content: The problem framing treats website boilerplate creation as a linear assembly of components, but this misses the fundamental systemic reality: a boilerplate is a seed system that evolves through projects. The sub-problems are correctly identified but their interactions create emergent properties. SP1 (functional requirements) and SP2 (technical architecture) form a tight feedback loop—technology choices constrain future functional possibilities, while functional needs push technology evolution. This creates path dependency: early technical decisions in the boilerplate will determine what kinds of websites can be efficiently built with it for years. SP3 (workflow) and SP5 (documentation) create another critical loop: the development workflow shapes what documentation is needed, while documentation quality determines how effectively the workflow can be adopted. The most significant oversight is treating the boilerplate as a static artifact rather than a living system with its own lifecycle. Each project using the boilerplate will create feedback—bugs, feature requests, performance data—that should flow back into boilerplate evolution. Without designing this feedback mechanism into SP3 and SP5, the boilerplate will gradually become obsolete as web standards and practices evolve. The assumption about 'modern web development' is particularly dangerous without defining what 'modern' means in a temporal system—today's modern stack becomes tomorrow's legacy system.
key_insights: The boilerplate's greatest value isn't in what it includes initially, but in its capacity for controlled evolution—the system needs versioning, deprecation pathways, and upgrade mechanisms designed in from the start.

Accessibility (SP4) and performance (Assumption 5) create conflicting systemic pressures—optimizing for one often degrades the other, requiring the boilerplate to include measurement and balancing tools rather than just baseline implementations.

The 'generalizable across common website types' constraint in SP1 creates a hidden anti-pattern: by trying to serve all purposes adequately, the boilerplate may serve none exceptionally, leading developers to strip it down rather than build upon it.

The documentation (SP5) isn't just an output—it's a control mechanism that shapes how the boilerplate propagates through an organization, creating cultural and practice norms that outlive any technical implementation.

tests.failure_mode: Even in optimal conditions, the empty solution set fails to provide any value. The boilerplate lacks essential components (HTML5 doctype, meta tags, CSS/JS structure, accessibility features, responsive design basics, SEO elements, security headers, performance optimizations). The solution provides zero functionality or guidance.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Immediately populate the solution set with actual boilerplate components. 2. Define minimum viable boilerplate: HTML5 structure, viewport meta tag, charset declaration, CSS reset/normalize, basic responsive container. 3. Add essential security headers template. 4. Include performance optimization placeholders (lazy loading, image optimization). 5. Document the boilerplate's intended use cases and limitations.

tests.failure_mode: Empty solution set provides no resilience to constraints. Without defined components, there's nothing to adapt when resources are limited, assumptions break, or data is partial. The solution collapses completely when faced with: limited bandwidth (no optimization strategies), outdated browsers (no polyfills or fallbacks), incomplete content (no error handling), or restricted hosting environments (no configuration options).
tests.recovery_path: 1. Define constraint-specific boilerplate variants (low-bandwidth version, legacy browser version). 2. Implement graceful degradation patterns. 3. Add resource loading prioritization. 4. Include offline capabilities and service worker skeleton. 5. Create modular component system where non-essential features can be removed without breaking core functionality.

tests.failure_mode: Empty solution is defenseless against adversarial attacks. Without security headers, input sanitization, CSP templates, or authentication patterns, the boilerplate enables every common web vulnerability: XSS (no output encoding), CSRF (no token implementation), injection attacks (no parameterized query patterns), clickjacking (no frame-busting code), information leakage (no security headers). An intelligent attacker would exploit the complete absence of security measures.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Implement mandatory security baseline: CSP headers, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options. 2. Add input validation and output encoding templates. 3. Include CSRF token implementation pattern. 4. Implement rate limiting and bot detection placeholders. 5. Add security monitoring and logging hooks. 6. Create security audit checklist as part of boilerplate documentation.
